question: Will the Bank of Russia decrease the key rate after the April Meeting?

fragility_points: A fresh inflation or expectations surprise before April 24 could justify a pause.
A strong independent analyst-consensus check could justify moving closer to market pricing.
key_assumptions: The March 20 cut reflects a real but conditional easing cycle rather than a one-off adjustment.
No material inflation reacceleration or external shock arrives before April 24.
Official conditional language should be taken literally rather than treated as a near-promise of another cut.
main_logical_chain: The contract resolves off the official April 24 Bank of Russia decision relative to the pre-meeting 15.00% rate.
The easing cycle is already underway, so a further April cut is the modal outcome.
But official communications keep the next move conditional, which means a hold remains a real tail risk.
Therefore the fair probability is high but below the market's near-certainty.
payload.main_thesis: Another Bank of Russia cut on April 24 is more likely than not because easing has already begun, but official materials still frame the move as conditional, so the market's 91.5% confidence looks too high.
quote_anchors: will assess the need for a further key rate cut at its upcoming meetings
some discussants noted that the data available were insufficient
payload.source_quality_view: Primary-source quality is high and source-of-truth ambiguity is low, but evidence independence is only medium because the core read relies heavily on Bank of Russia materials and secondary verification was light.
strongest_disconfirmers: The April 1 discussion summary preserves a plausible pause branch: some discussants said data were insufficient to confirm durable disinflation and warned consumption could reaccelerate.
The March release also stresses elevated inflation expectations and external uncertainty.
strongest_supports: The Bank of Russia already cut in February and March, taking the key rate to 15.00%.
The March 20 release explicitly says the Bank will assess the need for a further cut at upcoming meetings.
The April 1 discussion summary shows weaker-than-expected early-2026 activity and many officials seeing underlying inflation around 4-5% SAAR net of VAT distortions.
payload.timing_relevance: This view is specifically about the April 24, 2026 meeting and is most sensitive to any fresh inflation, expectations, or guidance signals released before then.
unresolved_ambiguities: I did not reconstruct a full independent historical frequency of consecutive Bank of Russia cuts from a clean time series in this run.
Independent pre-meeting consensus evidence for April 2026 was lighter than ideal.
payload.what_would_change_view: I would move higher on strong analyst-consensus or quasi-official guidance indicating another cut is the default, and lower on fresh data or communication implying March weakness was transitory and inflation risks are reasserting.

fragility_points: Any official evidence that February's disinflation was temporary would weaken the thesis quickly.
A fiscal or external shock before April 24 could shift the board toward pause.
Evidence base is high-quality but concentrated in the same institution.
key_assumptions: March's cut was the continuation of an easing sequence rather than a one-off adjustment.
Incoming April information will not overturn the recent disinflation/cooling-demand signal.
External and fiscal uncertainty remain cautionary but not binding enough to force a pause.
main_logical_chain: The market is effectively betting that March's easing resumes again in April.
Official March and April materials support further easing as the modal path but preserve explicit pause risk.
That makes a cut more likely than not, but not near-certain, so own probability sits below the 91.5% market price.
payload.main_thesis: Another April Bank of Russia rate cut is more likely than not, but the market's 91.5% confidence is too high because official guidance leaves meaningful pause risk.
quote_anchors: The Bank of Russia will assess the need for a further key rate cut at its upcoming meetings
this will not be done as a matter of course
payload.source_quality_view: High-quality official sources with low source-of-truth ambiguity, but only medium-low evidence independence because most evidence comes from the resolution authority itself.
strongest_disconfirmers: Nabiullina said further cuts would not happen as a matter of course.
Official discussion summary shows some participants thought data were insufficient to confirm durable disinflation.
External and fiscal uncertainty were repeatedly highlighted as reasons for caution.
strongest_supports: Bank of Russia already cut to 15.00% on March 20 and explicitly left open further cuts at upcoming meetings.
Official materials describe cooling demand and underlying inflation around 4-5% SAAR after tax distortions.
April 24 decision release plus medium-term forecast is the dominant repricing catalyst before resolution.
payload.timing_relevance: The April 24 Bank of Russia decision package is the key catalyst and likely repricing moment; softer pre-meeting signals matter mainly insofar as they change expectations for that event.
unresolved_ambiguities: How the board weighs elevated inflation expectations versus realized disinflation by April 24.
Whether policymakers see one meeting as too soon after March for another cut.
How much any fiscal-rule changes alter the inflation outlook before the meeting.
payload.what_would_change_view: I would cut the estimate if official data or messaging show reaccelerating inflation, worsening fiscal/external risks, or explicit preference to wait beyond April; I would raise it if official April information strongly confirms durable target-consistent disinflation.

fragility_points: New inflation or FX data before 24 April could shift the decision.
Fiscal-policy uncertainty remains unresolved in the official discussion.
The decisive evidence comes from one institution, so the view is sensitive to its late messaging.
key_assumptions: The March disinflation and demand-cooling story largely remains intact into the 24 April meeting.
No major inflation, ruble, or fiscal shock arrives before the meeting.
The market is mostly extrapolating official easing signals rather than hidden contrary information.
main_logical_chain: Start from the 91.5% market prior because the central bank has already been easing.
Check the official March decision, April discussion summary, and governor statement for what policy makers actually signaled.
Those sources support another cut as the base case but preserve a real pause path, so trim the market probability modestly rather than rejecting it.
payload.main_thesis: The market is directionally right that another April Bank of Russia cut is the base case, but 91.5% looks somewhat overconfident; a conditional official pause path keeps my estimate at 84%.
quote_anchors: the need for a further key rate cut at its upcoming meetings
will not be done as a matter of course
payload.source_quality_view: High-quality primary evidence with low source-of-truth ambiguity, but only medium independence because the key evidence is mostly official Bank of Russia communication.
strongest_disconfirmers: Official communication repeatedly emphasized external and fiscal uncertainty.
Some discussants said the data were insufficient to conclude inflation had durably cooled.
Nabiullina said further cuts would not be done as a matter of course.
strongest_supports: Bank of Russia cut 50 bp on 20 March and said it would assess the need for further cuts at upcoming meetings.
Official materials described underlying inflation around 4-5% annualised and weaker demand.
The April 1 discussion summary showed most participants saw conditions broadly consistent with continued easing.
payload.timing_relevance: High; this is a date-specific central-bank decision market with roughly 10 days to resolution and potentially meaningful interim official or macro signals.
unresolved_ambiguities: Whether early-2026 demand weakness is temporary payback or more durable cooling.
How much weight the Bank will put on external and fiscal risks by the April meeting.
payload.what_would_change_view: A fresh official pause signal, hotter inflation, higher inflation expectations, ruble weakness, or more proinflationary fiscal news would lower my cut probability; explicit renewed easing guidance would raise it.

fragility_points: A single hawkish shift in official tone before April 24 could reopen meaningful pause risk.
The market may be over-extrapolating policy continuity from one cut plus conditional guidance.
Evidence independence is limited because the directional case relies mainly on official central-bank communications.
key_assumptions: The March 2026 cut was the start of a near-term easing sequence rather than a one-off calibration move.
No intervening inflation or external-risk shock will be serious enough to force a pause by 24 April.
main_logical_chain: The latest official policy decision already began easing with a 50 bp cut in March.
That same official release preserved room for a further cut at upcoming meetings, making April the base case.
But the same release preserved meaningful pause risk through inflation-expectations and external-risk caveats.
Therefore the market direction looks right, but the 91.5% confidence looks somewhat too high.
payload.main_thesis: An April Bank of Russia rate cut remains the base case, but the market is pricing too much certainty into a still-conditional easing path.
quote_anchors: will assess the need for a further key rate cut at its upcoming meetings
uncertainty regarding the external environment has increased considerably
proinflationary risks still prevail over disinflationary ones
payload.source_quality_view: High-quality official sources for both policy signal and settlement mechanics, but limited independence for directional inference.
strongest_disconfirmers: The March release also said external-environment uncertainty had increased considerably.
The March release said proinflationary risks still prevailed over disinflationary ones.
The contract resolves only on the April 24 decision, so broader easing-cycle logic can still fail on timing.
strongest_supports: The Bank of Russia cut by 50 bp on 20 March 2026.
The March release explicitly said it would assess the need for a further cut at upcoming meetings.
The March release described inflation as slowing and activity as cooling.
payload.timing_relevance: High: this is a date-specific meeting market, so the question is the April 24 decision itself, not the broader easing cycle.
unresolved_ambiguities: How heavily the Board will weight external-environment risks by April 24.
Whether inflation expectations remain benign enough to support a second consecutive cut.
payload.what_would_change_view: Official April-period communication that either clearly reinforces continued easing with fewer caveats or instead signals that March was sufficient and that inflation/external risks argue for a pause.

fragility_points: Evidence independence is limited because the contextual source likely reflects the same official narrative.
A single new official data or communication signal before April 24 could compress the pause-risk thesis.
key_assumptions: The market is over-extrapolating the March 2026 cut.
The Bank of Russia could pause in April without ending the broader easing cycle.
No pre-meeting official data shock emerges that clearly forces an April cut.
main_logical_chain: The market implies 91.5% for an April decrease.
Official sources confirm the current baseline is 15.00% and that the April 24 Bank of Russia release is the governing settlement source.
Contextual evidence supports an ongoing easing cycle but also preserves inflation-risk reasons for a pause.
Therefore Yes remains the base case, but the probability should be materially below near-certainty because an April hold is still plausible.
payload.main_thesis: The Bank of Russia is still more likely than not to cut in April, but the market is too confident because a one-meeting pause after the March cut is a live underweighted risk.
quote_anchors: 24 April 2026 ... Press release on the key rate
payload.source_quality_view: High confidence in settlement mechanics from official Bank of Russia pages; only medium confidence in directional inference because contextual evidence independence is limited.
strongest_disconfirmers: If March was the seventh consecutive cut and disinflation is real, another immediate April cut may simply continue the established cycle.
strongest_supports: Official Bank of Russia key-rate database shows the pre-meeting rate is already 15.00%.
Contextual macro source frames March as part of an easing cycle but still notes proinflationary risks.
This contract is only about the April meeting outcome, so timing risk matters more than the broader path.
payload.timing_relevance: High, because the contract resolves only on the April meeting and a temporary pause would still resolve against the broader easing narrative.
unresolved_ambiguities: Independent preview coverage was sparse in available tooling.
The future official press-release page exists structurally but does not yet expose decision text.
payload.what_would_change_view: I would move toward the market on clear official or broadly independent preview evidence that disinflation is ahead of plan and an April cut is overwhelmingly expected with little pause discussion.
